title: Two kinds of equal
why.untouched: script.js is exactly as it was.
hint2.untouched: The code gives the right answers. All three comparisons use two equals signs.
why.not-closed: A tag in index.html is open and never closed.
why.not-linked: The script never reaches the page.
why.crashed: Your code stopped with an error.
hint2.crashed: Look at the Console panel below.
hint2.changed-data: That is how it arrives from the field. The code has to cope, not the data change.
why.loose-left: Somewhere you are still comparing with two signs.
hint2.loose-left: All three places get rewritten. Where the types differ, convert explicitly.
why.wrong-warning: The warning does not come out.
hint2.wrong-warning: The customer typed zero cups. With three signs the text "0" and the number 0 are not the same thing.
hint3.wrong-warning: Convert the text to a number before comparing. Then the comparison is between two numbers and it is honest.
why.wrong-code: The missing code is not spotted.
hint2.wrong-code: The field is empty. Compare it with empty text, not with false.
hint3.wrong-code: "" == false is true, but by accident. Empty text is not falsehood — it is empty text.
hint2.wrong-empty: cups is already a number. Compare it with a number.
why.wrong-count: There are not three lines in the console.
why.lost-p: A paragraph has disappeared from the page.
blocks.0.text: Three checks on what a customer typed. All three give the right answer.
blocks.1.text: And all three are written in a way that will betray you the moment the data changes slightly.
blocks.3.text: When the two sides are different types, two equals signs bring them to a common type and only then compare. Three signs do not do that — a different type means different, full stop.
blocks.5.text: All three give true. The first compares text with a number. The second compares text with a boolean. The third compares two different things that both mean no value.
blocks.7.text: You will meet the claim that two signs are broken, banned, or a leftover mistake. They are not. Their rules are written into the language standard and every browser has followed them identically for thirty years.
blocks.8.text: The problem is not that they lie. The problem is that their rules are a long table nobody remembers — and code that relies on an unremembered table is code that will surprise you.
blocks.10.text: This is the special case, and it is DELIBERATE, not an oversight. The standard explicitly says the two are equal to each other and to nothing else.
blocks.11.text: There is logic to it: both mean there is no value here, for different reasons. undefined means nobody ever put anything. null means somebody explicitly put nothing.
blocks.12.quote: The one place two signs are useful: checking whether something is missing, either way.
blocks.14.text: Write three signs everywhere. Not because two are evil, but because three signs mean one thing and two signs mean a table.
blocks.15.text: If the types differ — convert yourself, explicitly, before comparing. Then the code shows what is happening instead of hiding it in a rule.
blocks.17.text: Rewrite the three comparisons with three signs. The answers must stay the same.
blocks.18.text: In one place you will have to convert. In another — compare with the right thing, rather than with false.
